senses.definition: past tense of slack, meaning to become less active, to decline, or to cause something to become looser or less tight
examples: She slacked off on her studying during the exam period.
The rope slacked as the load was lowered.

senses.definition: to become less tight or to reduce in effort or speed
examples: If you pull too hard, the rope will slacken.
He tends to slacken his pace when the crowd grows quiet.

senses.definition: past tense of slacken; became looser or less tight, or became less active
examples: The tension slackened after the accident was cleared.
Her grip slackened and the pen fell.

senses.definition: a thing that slackens; a device or person that causes looseness or reduces tension
examples: The slackener on the cable is damaged.
He considered himself a slackener of procedures.

senses.definition: plural of slackener; items or people that cause looseness or reduced tension
examples: The slackeners were replaced to restore proper tension.
There are several slackeners in the system.

senses.definition: the act or process of becoming looser or less intense; a decrease in effort
examples: There was a slackening of tempo in the music.
The team's slackening of pace worried the coach.

senses.definition: plural of slackening; instances of becoming looser or less intense
examples: Several slackenings were observed during the trial.
The slackenings varied in duration.

senses.definition: third-person singular present tense of slacken; to become looser or to reduce effort
examples: The rope slackens when the load is lowered.
His grip slackens as fatigue sets in.

senses.definition: a person who is lazy or avoids work
examples: Don't be a slacker; help finish the chores.
The class accused him of being a total slacker.

senses.definition: the behavior or attitude of slacking; habit of avoiding work
examples: His slackerism frustrated his team.
Slackerism can hurt a project’s progress.

senses.definition: plural of slacker; multiple lazy people
examples: The group warned the slackers to join the effort.
Two slackers were asked to leave the team.

senses.definition: superlative form of slacky or slack; most loose or least active
examples: This is the slackest part of the rope.
He was the slackest student in the class.

senses.definition: informal, affectionate term for a lazy or slack person; can be playful or mildly insulting
examples: Quit being a slackie and finish your homework.
He jokes that he’s a total slackie on Sundays.

senses.definition: present participle of slack; avoiding work or reducing effort
examples: The workers are slacking off instead of finishing the job.
She caught herself slacking and refocused.

senses.definition: in a way that is slacking or lazy; without proper effort
examples: He spoke slackingly, without much interest.
She slackedingly completed the task, barely finishing.

senses.definition: in a slack or loose manner; not tight or straining
examples: The rope hung slackly between the posts.
Her grip lay slackly on the handle.

senses.definition: having a slack or lazy mindset; not careful or diligent
examples: His slackminded approach led to mistakes.
A slackminded attitude won't help in exams.

senses.definition: the state of being slackminded; lack of focus or diligence
examples: Her slackmindedness caused missing deadlines.
A culture of slackmindedness can damage productivity.

senses.definition: the state of being slack; lack of effort or tightness
examples: There is slackness in the rope.
The slackness of discipline worried the coach.

senses.definition: plural of slackness; multiple instances of looseness or laxity
examples: The rope had several slacknesses along its length.
The report highlighted various financial slacknesses.

senses.definition: informal trousers for casual wear; loosely fitting pants
examples: He bought a new pair of slacks for the weekend.
Her beige slacks matched the shirt nicely.

senses.definition: actions that support a political or social cause mainly through online activity rather than real-world effort
examples: Many people engage in slacktivism by liking posts but not donating.
The campaign criticized slacktivism as insufficient for real change.

senses.definition: plural of slacktivism; online actions supporting causes with little real-world impact
examples: The criticisms of slacktivisms grew louder during the campaign.
Skeptics argued that slacktivisms rarely lead to policy change.

senses.definition: a person who supports a cause primarily through online activity with little real-world action
examples: Some people call him a slacktivist because he shares posts but never volunteers.
Critics say slacktivists fail to create real change.

senses.definition: plural of slacktivist; multiple people who mainly act online for a cause
examples: The event attracted many slacktivists who posted but did not participate in person.
Advocates argued that slacktivists still raise awareness.

senses.definition: a person who supports a cause mainly through online activities rather than real-world actions
examples: Don't be a slacktivist; join us on the ground too.
The article described slacktivists as a mixed blessing.

senses.definition: plural form referring to people who engage in slacktivism
examples: The conference challenged slacktivists to take tangible actions.
Media debates whether slacktivists harm or help movements.
